content.problemStatement:
For a given array BBB, we can divide the array into 222 **non-empty** subsequences, such that, each element of the array is present in **exactly** one of the subsequences.

Let XXX and YYY denote the GCD of all elements of the first and second subsequence respectively.

We define the maximum diversity of the array BBB as:

MXDV(B)=max(X‚àíY)MXDV(B) = max(X - Y)MXDV(B)=max(X‚àíY), over all possible pairs of subsequences.

Given an array AAA of NNN non-negative integers‚Äã and a non-negative integer KKK, find the length of the **smallest** subarray SSS of array AAA such that MXDV(S)‚â•KMXDV(S) \geq KMXDV(S)‚â•K.

If no such subarray exists, print ‚àí1-1‚àí1.


Input Format

First line contains TTT ‚àí the number of test cases. The description of the test cases is as follows.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN, KKK ‚àí the length of array AAA and the value of KKK.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã‚Äã.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer ‚àí the length of the **smallest** subarray SSS of array AAA such that MXDV(S)‚â•KMXDV(S) \geq KMXDV(S)‚â•K. If no such subarray exists, print ‚àí1-1‚àí1.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 10510^5105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One possible subarray SSS can be [3,9][3, 9][3,9] and the subsequences thus formed can be [9][9][9], [3][3][3]. The value of X‚àíY=9‚àí3=6X - Y = 9 - 3 = 6X‚àíY=9‚àí3=6. It can be shown that this is the smallest array for which MXDV(S)=6‚â•5MXDV(S) = 6 \geq 5MXDV(S)=6‚â•5.
Test case 222: One of the possible subarray SSS can be [10,3,9][10, 3, 9][10,3,9] and the subsequences thus formed can be [9][9][9], [10,3][10, 3][10,3]. The value of X‚àíY=9‚àí1=8X - Y= 9 - 1 = 8X‚àíY=9‚àí1=8. It can be proven that there is no subarray SSS with less than 333 elements which can have MXDV(S)=8‚â•8MXDV(S) = 8\geq 8MXDV(S)=8‚â•8.
